s2.q: The TORCH acronym in obstetrics stands for:
options: Toxoplasma, Rubella, CMV, Herpes, and Others
s2.explain: TORCH stands for Toxoplasma, Others (Parvovirus B19, VZV, measles, enteroviruses, HIV), Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, and Herpes simplex. These are infections known to produce congenital defects.

s2.q: Infections during pregnancy can develop in the neonate by all of the following routes EXCEPT:
s2.explain: Infections can be transmitted transplacentally, perinatally (from vaginal secretions or blood), and postnatally (from breast milk or other sources). Immunizations are not a route of infection transmission.

s2.q: Which of the following is included under 'Others' in the TORCH acronym?
s2.explain: 'Others' in TORCH includes Parvovirus B19 (B19V), varicella-zoster virus (VZV), measles virus, enteroviruses, and human immunodeficiency virus (HIV).

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.
